tone_and_voice: Internal, HR/leadership-facing, and confidential in register -- distinctly different from customer-facing reports since the audience (leadership) is also, in a sense, the subject of some findings (e.g. management transparency). Must balance candor about morale issues with a constructive, action-oriented framing.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must identify the single most-cited theme with clear numeric separation from other themes (e.g. 'roughly double the next concern') to give leadership an unambiguous first priority, since diffuse engagement action plans typically fail.
section_rules.discussion: Should explicitly note the value of company-wide (multi-office) participation in strengthening confidence that a finding is organizational rather than one office's local issue.
section_rules.methodology: State whether the survey was anonymous, since anonymity materially affects candor in employee engagement data and should be noted for reader confidence in findings.
section_rules.limitations: Note response rate relative to total eligible staff, since low participation can indicate either survey fatigue or that only particularly engaged/disengaged staff responded, skewing findings.
section_rules.recommendations: Must include a specific staff communication/feedback-loop recommendation, since engagement surveys not followed by visible action are known to depress future participation and trust.
section_rules.policy_implications: Frame as 'organizational policy implications' (e.g. HR policy, management practice) rather than public policy implications.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Should reflect on whether the open-ended format surfaced organization-wide priorities more clearly than a fixed-dimension rating scale would have.
section_rules.conclusion: State the specific leading concern and a commitment to visible follow-up -- an employee engagement report without a stated action commitment risks undermining trust in the exercise itself.
section_rules.annexes: Include response rate by office/region if available, to support confidence that findings reflect the whole organization rather than one location.
section_rules.evidence_style: Present sentiment and theme frequency data plainly; avoid quoting specific verbatim employee comments in ways that could risk identifying an individual respondent, even anonymized -- paraphrase where a quote might be too specific to a small team or role.

common_kpis: leading concern theme frequency (with numeric separation from other themes)
negative sentiment share (tracked explicitly, not absorbed into overall positive framing)
common_risks: low response rate skewing toward particularly engaged or disengaged staff
lack of follow-up communication depressing future survey participation
verbatim quotes risking respondent identifiability even when anonymized
typical_recommendations: visible leadership communication acknowledging survey findings
targeted action plan for the leading concern with trackable milestones
shorter-interval pulse surveys on the leading concern specifically
typical_policy_implications: HR policy and management practice adjustments
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Not applicable -- internal HR/leadership audience; equivalent expectation is visible commitment to action, since engagement data without follow-through is a well-documented driver of declining trust in internal surveys.

forbidden_behaviors: Never quote a verbatim employee comment specific enough to risk identifying an individual, even when the survey is nominally anonymous.
Never present a low response rate as if it were fully representative of the whole organization without noting this limitation.
Never use customer-facing or development-sector terminology in an internal staff engagement report.
